coredata.dc:title: One pot synthesis of cobalt and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots based fluorescence ‘On-Off-On’ probe for dimethoate sensing: A proof of concept
coredata.dc:creator: Mahajan, Mahendra R.
Patil, Pravin O.
coredata.dc:description: Dimethoate, which is widely used in agriculture, has harmful effects on organs. To address this issue, advanced sensors are crucial for detecting it in real samples. Notably, graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-centered fluorescent sensors offer numerous advantages such as high sensitivity, selectivity, and good stability. Therefore, in this work, we designed a new cobalt/nitrogen co-doped GQD (Co/N-GQD) fluorescence switch “On-Off-On” sensor to achieve selective dimethoate detection. In brief, the synthesis of Co/N-GQDs, achieved through the hydrothermal method, involves Pithecellobium dulce fruit peel as a green precursor, along with cobalt (Co) and urea as dopant sources. Extensive spectral characterizations, including FTIR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, particle size analysis, HR-TEM, Raman, PXRD, and fluorescence studies, were conducted to validate the fabrication of Co/N-GQDs. The resulting nanosized Co/N-GQDs exhibited an enhanced quantum yield of 49.78 %. In terms of sensing, the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs is selectively quenched (“Turned Off”) by Cu2+ through a dynamic quenching mechanism. When dimethoate is included in the quenching system, a proportional correlation is observed between dimethoate concentration and fluorescence reactivation. This phenomenon is attributed to the potential of dimethoate, which contains amide and phosphorodithioate functionalities, to displace Cu2+ from the electrostatic complex through chelation. Remarkably, the sensor achieves a limit detection limit (LOD) of 64.08 ng/mL, offering a broad linear range spanning from 10 to 800 ng/mL. In addition, it exhibited real-time applicability, good stability, and reproducibility. In conclusion, the design of Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs can be used as a proof of concept for dimethoate sensing within various sample contexts.

Additionally, heartfelt acknowledgments are extended to the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT)–Roorkee, India, and the Sophisticated Test and Instrumentation Centre (STIC) at Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, Kerala, India, for providing essential resources. Lastly, the author appreciates Dr. S. B. Bari, principal, and the management of H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, for offering the necessary amenities.

head.ce:title: One pot synthesis of cobalt and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots based fluorescence ‘On-Off-On’ probe for dimethoate sensing: A proof of concept

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, Dist., Dhule, MS 425405, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, Dist., Dhule, MS 425405, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dimethoate, which is widely used in agriculture, has harmful effects on organs. To address this issue, advanced sensors are crucial for detecting it in real samples. Notably, graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-centered fluorescent sensors offer numerous advantages such as high sensitivity, selectivity, and good stability. Therefore, in this work, we designed a new cobalt/nitrogen co-doped GQD (Co/N-GQD) fluorescence switch “On-Off-On” sensor to achieve selective dimethoate detection. In brief, the synthesis of Co/N-GQDs, achieved through the hydrothermal method, involves Pithecellobium dulce fruit peel as a green precursor, along with cobalt (Co) and urea as dopant sources. Extensive spectral characterizations, including FTIR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, particle size analysis, HR-TEM, Raman, PXRD, and fluorescence studies, were conducted to validate the fabrication of Co/N-GQDs. The resulting nanosized Co/N-GQDs exhibited an enhanced quantum yield of 49.78 %. In terms of sensing, the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs is selectively quenched (“Turned Off”) by Cu2+ through a dynamic quenching mechanism. When dimethoate is included in the quenching system, a proportional correlation is observed between dimethoate concentration and fluorescence reactivation. This phenomenon is attributed to the potential of dimethoate, which contains amide and phosphorodithioate functionalities, to displace Cu2+ from the electrostatic complex through chelation. Remarkably, the sensor achieves a limit detection limit (LOD) of 64.08 ng/mL, offering a broad linear range spanning from 10 to 800 ng/mL. In addition, it exhibited real-time applicability, good stability, and reproducibility. In conclusion, the design of Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs can be used as a proof of concept for dimethoate sensing within various sample contexts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In today’s modern era, organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) play an important role in crop protection globally. These pesticides are highly regarded for their potent insecticidal action, water solubility, and rapid degradation [1]. Despite their benefits, long-term OPP presence in agriculture, soil, and water threatens health and survival [2]. Dimethoate, a thio-organophosphate, finds global use but is banned in Europe due to harm [3]. Still, it is used in parts of Asia, Africa, Brazil, and the USA, raising significant concerns about its potential risks and effects [4]. Dimethoate exerts its toxicity by irreversibly inhibiting the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE), leading to organ dysfunction and death [5]. In alignment with this, the World Health Organization (WHO) has established a supremely acceptable dose of dimethoate at 0.002 mg/kg of total body weight [6]. This chemical can harm multiple organs [7]. Hence, it is crucial to closely monitor and control the presence of OPPs in food, water, and the environment, demanding special attention to each of these aspects. Numerous conventional techniques have been explored for monitoring dimethoate, such as spectrophotometry [8], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [9], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [10], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], and gas chromatography (GC) [12]. These methods offer highly sensitive, selective, and quantitative monitoring of dimethoate. However, they come with inherent drawbacks such as expensive equipment, complex operations, time-consuming pre-treatment, and the need for experienced operators [13]. Consequently, the academic and scientific community is closely focusing on alternative sensor technologies to track various pesticides. In brief, inorganic quantum dots, such as cadmium telluride/cadmium sulfide-based sensors, have been utilized for monitoring OPPs. However, they have some drawbacks in terms of sensitivity and toxicity [14]. In addition, fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots face challenges due to complex preparation processes, expensive chemicals, and additional toxicity from heavy metals, which restrict their widespread usage [15].

In the realm of pesticide detection, the optical platform has garnered significant attention for the detection of OPPs due to its notable advantages in terms of sensitivity and selectivity. Previous studies have explored several nanomaterials to detect dimethoate, including gold [16] and silver-based nanomaterials [17], quantum dots [18], carbon nanotubes [19], carbon dots (CDs) [20], and metal–organic frameworks [6]. However, it is imperative to acknowledge that most dual-mode sensors, which combine colorimetry and fluorescence, incorporate optical components such as metal nanoclusters, metal-doped nanomaterials, and fluorescent dyes. Despite their promising potential, these sensors face challenges related to low biocompatibility, complex synthesis procedures, the use of expensive reagents, lower quantum yield, and stability issues in aqueous environments. The presence of these challenges emphasizes the need for research and development activities focused on enhancing the efficiency and feasibility of dimethoate detection methods. In contrast, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based fluorescence sensors offer cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid processing, making them ideal for pesticide detection [21]. Recently, fluorescence-dependent zero-dimensional GQDs have been proposed as novel probes for OPP detection in water and food. Since its inception, GQD has captured the curiosity of the scientific circle due to its peculiar optical, mechanical, and electrical qualities. In addition, biocompatibility, good photostability, tunable photoluminescence, water solubility, and a wide surface area [22]. Broadly, the development of GQDs has been pursued through both bottom-up and top-down approaches. Nowadays, green natural precursors are being used to prepare fluorescent GQDs to overcome the limitations of labor-intensive chemical processes. Usually, GQD-based fluorescent sensors detect analytes via quenching (static/dynamic), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) mechanisms, employing “On-Off” or “Off-On” strategies. Unfortunately, the QY of bare GQDs is lower than that of typical QDs, which limits their wide applicability of QDs. In this regard, increasing QY is one of the major challenges in the implementation of GQDs [23]. Currently, researchers are directing their efforts toward surface functionalization and heteroatom-doped strategies to enhance the physical, chemical, and optical properties of GQDs. By doing so, the aim is to improve QY, alter the intrinsic characteristics of GQDs, and enhance their performance [24].

While the nonmetal element doping of nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) in GQDs for pesticide detection has already been explored [25], the study of metal doping in GQDs is still relatively new and unexplored. Therefore, our work focuses on the design of Co/N-GQDs as fluorescent nanomaterials for dimethoate monitoring. In particular, the introduction of ‘N’ doping holds the potential for boosting GQDs' high QY, fluorescence stability, and photoluminescence. [26]. Over recent years, ‘Co’ has emerged as an exceptionally remarkable and versatile element, captivating researcher's attention due to its affordability and exceptional magnetic, electrical, optical, and electrochemical properties [27]. Similarly, ‘Co’ doping has emerged as a remarkably versatile and important approach in various fields, owing to its low toxicity and wide availability. In addition, ‘Co’ doping in fluorescent nanomaterials provides a reliable platform to consistently enhance their electrical and optical properties [28]. Zhang et al. demonstrated that ‘Co’ decoration on N-doped carbon materials establishes a robust coordination relationship. The presence of ‘N’ doping offers active binding sites for ‘Co’ metal (Co-Nx) within the carbon matrix, leading to synergistic effects that enhance the electrical properties of the material [29]. Fundamentally, doping zero-dimensional nanomaterials with metal ions prompts substantial shifts in electron distribution and energy gap, reshaping their electrical, magnetic, and optical attributes. This is propelled by the distinctive electron surplus, empty orbitals, and larger atomic size of metal ions in contrast to nonmetals [30]. Incorporating both metal and nonmetal dopants into GQDs introduces new defect sites, resulting in an increase in unoccupied energy levels and an improvement in electron donation/withdrawal quality due to the synergistic effect of the dopants [31]. Metal dopants significantly modify the band structure of fluorescent nanomaterials, thereby enhancing the fluorescence characteristics of GQDs [32]. Notably, codoped GQDs with active defect sites, such as carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups, exhibit heightened binding energy and enhanced charge transfer characteristics. These interactions, particularly through hydrogen bonding with the surface of the phosphate group of pesticides, make codoped GQDs particularly promising for sensing applications [25]. This preference for doped GQDs opens new avenues for the detection of dimethoate, offering improved analytical parameters such as sensitivity, selectivity, real-time applicability, and more. To the best of our knowledge, there is no existing report on the detection of dimethoate using Co/N-GQDs in this context.

Therefore, this research work introduces a novel fluorescence sensor based on Co/N-GQDs designed to detect dimethoate with high sensitivity, selectivity, practical applicability, stability, and more. In brief, the sensor was created through a hydrothermal approach involving the use of Pithecellobium dulce fruit peel as a green precursor and ‘Co’ and urea as dopant sources for ‘Co’, and ‘N’, respectively. Various spectral techniques, such as FTIR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, particle size analysis, HR-TEM, Raman, PXRD, and fluorescence studies, were employed to confirm the fabrication of fluorescence Co/N-GQDs. The introduction of an optimal concentration of Cu2+ into the Co/N-GQDs solution led to fluorescence quenching, referred to as “Turn-Off,” due to the formation of an electrostatic complex. Subsequent addition of dimethoate to the quenched system resulted in the proportional recovery of fluorescence, termed “Turn-On.” This revival of fluorescence was attributed to dimethoate displacing Cu2+ from the electrostatic complex, leveraging its amide and phosphorodithioate functionalities with a strong chelating affinity for Cu2+. In essence, the designed nanosized Co/N-GQDs fluorescence sensor offers remarkable sensitivity, selectivity, and stability for dimethoate detection. Shortly, Co/N-GQDs hold the potential to identify dimethoate in water and food samples.

The Pithecellobium dulce fruit peel was gathered from Shirpur, Dhule district (Maharashtra), India. Chemical compounds such as dimethoate, thiram, glyphosate, acephate, malathion, monocrotophos, methyl parathion, carbendazim, copper sulfate, urea, cobalt (II) nitrate Co (NO3)2, mercuric chloride (HgCl2), zinc nitrate Zn (NO3)2, lead nitrate Pb (NO3)2, cadium chloride (CdCl2) and phosphate buffer capsule (with pH levels of 7.4 ± 0.05) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Private Limited, India. Additionally, sodium hydroxide was obtained from Merck Specialties Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India, and sodium chloride, magnesium sulfate, quinine sulfate, ferric nitrate Fe (NO3)3, potassium chloride, ferrous nitrate Fe (NO3)2 and was acquired from Loba Chemie Chemicals Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. Acquisition of double-distilled water (DDW) was facilitated through Avtar Performance Materials India Limited, an establishment based in Thane, Maharashtra. All solvents employed held analytical credentials and were used as is, without further purification.

The synthesized Co/N-GQDs were subjected to analysis using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV1800 Shimadzu, Japan) within the wavelength range of 400 to 800 nm, employing a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence characteristics of Co/N-GQDs were evaluated at pH values ranging from 4.0 to 9.0 using a UV chamber (Southern Scientific Lab Instrument, Chennai, India) and wavelengths of 254 nm, 365 nm, and visible light. The fluorescence measurements of Co/N-GQDs in the 200 to 750 nm range were observed using a Jasco fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-8200). Moreover, by employing attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) via a Bruker Alpha II device, an examination of the Co/N-GQDs was undertaken, encompassing wavelengths across the spectrum from 600 to 3500 nm. For X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, facilities at the Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, India, were utilized. The crystallinity assessment of the fabricated materials was performed through X-ray powder diffractometry (XRD) using a Bruker D8 Advance instrument at BITS Pilani, Goa, India. The external morphology and elemental composition of the synthesized Co/N-GQDs were investigated using scanning electron microscopy combined with an energy-dispersive X-ray detector (SEM-EDAX) Quanta FEG 250 at BITS Pilani, Goa, India. The LaB6 illumination source operating at 200 kV, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed using a Jeol/JEM 2100 instrument to determine the size, shape, and morphology of the green-synthesized Co/N-GQDs. Zeta potential determination particle size analysis and polydispersity index (PDI) of the synthesized Co/N-GQDs were performed using a Nanoplus 3 particulate system (Micromeritics, USA). Raman analysis was conducted using a Lab RAM HR Horiba France confocal micro-Raman spectrometer (BITS Pilani, Goa, India) with a 532-nm laser operating at 100 mW power at room temperature.

In this work, the luminescence QY of Co/N-GQDs was computed using a comparative method. Significantly, quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (st = 54 %) as a benchmark for QY determination. Subsequently, dilutions were prepared using double distilled water (DDW). Similarly, absorbance was using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 340 nm, and optical density values were maintained below 0.1 to minimize the impact of reabsorption. Next, the fluorescence emission spectra of quinine sulfate and Co/N-GQDs were captured at 340 nm excitation, and a graph illustrating the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance was generated. The 'QY' of Co/N-doped GQDs was determined using the subsequent Eq. (1).
(1)
Q Y x = Q Y s t
G r a d
x
G r a d
s t
η x
η
s t
Here, ‘QY’ represents the quantum yield, where 'x' and 'st' represent Co/N-GQDs (experimental) and quinine sulfate (standard), respectively. The gradient of the fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curve is denoted as ‘grade’, while ‘η’ represents the refractive index of the solvent [34].

In essence, existing research indicates that pH actively modifies the fluorescence characteristics of nanoprobes. Mainly, pH affects the surface stability and molecularity of the surface functional groups of the sensors. Therefore, the effect of pH on the luminescent properties of Co/N-GQDs was investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometry using buffers with pH values ranging from 4.0 to 9.0. Briefly, to prepare a stock solution for this shade, 10 mg of Co/N-GQDs was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water (100 μg/mL). Furthermore, a dilution series was prepared from this stock solution. In which 10 μL (0.01 mL) of the solution was added to 10 mL of deionized water and various pH-buffered solutions. The fluorescence emission of the Co/N-GQDs solution was monitored at numerous excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 360 nm. In addition, Co/N-GQDs solutions with different pH values were studied in a UV fluorescence cabinet to evaluate the inverse variation in fluorescence intensity as a function of wavelength. We then investigated the fluorescence emission characteristics of the prepared Co/N-GQDs even after 12 h of incubation in different pH solutions. In this, the effect of pH influences the obvious changes in the luminescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs and reveals a fluorescence maximum of 7.4 when probed at 340 nm excitation wavelength. The effect of pH on the zeta potential deviation of Co/N-GQDs solutions was monitored. This study sheds light on the protonation and deprotonation processes occurring in Co/N-GQDs external functional groups and understands the surface chemistry and stability of the probe.

In this typical experiment, dimethoate was monitored at pH 7.4 using a (JASCO FP8200 spectrofluorometer). Initially, 1 mg of freshly synthesized Co/N-GQDs was dissolved in 10 mL of pH 7.4 phosphate buffer (100 μg/mL). Then, the prepared Co/N-GQDs probe solution was placed in a quartz cuvette. The sample was illuminated in a scanning spectrum from 290 to 360 nm at 22 °C, keeping the slit width constant at 5 nm for both the excitation and emission spectra. To examine the quenching efficiency of the Co/N-GQD nanoprobe, various concentrations of Cu2+ ions ranging from 1 to 900 ng/mL were prepared in a pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solution. These Cu2+ ions were then added to the fluorescent Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes. The resulting quenching efficiency was determined for different Cu2+ ion concentrations. Once the optimal Cu2+ ion concentrations were identified, dimethoate detection was accomplished. For dimethoate detection, a dimethoate sample with a concentration of 1 ng/mL was loaded into the Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe sensor. The probe mixture was then set aside at 25 °C for 5 to 20 min to allow fluorescence recovery. The fluorescence recovery of the quenched nanoprobe was eventually determined. In addition, different dimethoate concentrations ranging from 10 to 800 ng/mL were tested for fluorescence recovery. These data were used to create a Stern-Volmer plot using the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe fluorescence intensity and dimethoate concentration. To calculate the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) for dimethoate, Eqs.
(2) and (3) for dimethoate were calculated using slope (m) and standard deviation (ϭ).
(2)
L O D = 3.3
σ m
(3)
L O Q = 10
σ m
Furthermore, the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe exhibited enhanced analytical attributes, including stability and reproducibility. The stability evaluation of the Co/N-GQDs sensor involved maintaining a consistent concentration of 800 ng/mL dimethoate over six days at 25 °C. During this phase, the fluorescence response of the prepared Co/N-GQDs was meticulously scrutinized. This was followed by the computation of the percentage relative standard deviation (% RSD). In addition, the stability of the Co/N-GQDs was meticulously evaluated at distinct time intervals. This comprehensive analysis involved studying the recuperation of fluorescence from the quenched state of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe. Subsequently, the luminescence recovery of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe was quantified (n = 6), culminating in the calculation of the % RSD to elucidate the stability of the nanoprobe in the context of dimethoate detection. To assess reproducibility, Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes were employed with a dimethoate concentration of 800 ng/mL (n = 6). To confirm the reliability of the results, the % recovery of fluorescence and % RSD were meticulously determined.

To assess the practical applicability and performance of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe in dimethoate recognition. We chose tap water, and fruits (apples, oranges, and lemons) as real samples. For tap water, samples were collected from locations around the campus and then filtered using Whatman filter paper to obtain actual samples. A fruit was purchased from a vegetable market in Shirpur (Maharashtra) and used as a real sample. To simulate real conditions, the fruit surface was sprayed with dimethoate and kept at room temperature for two days. After the incubation period, the edible part of the fruit was carefully cut off. Then 20 g sample of cut fruit was mixed with 50 mL water and stirred for 30 min. Finally, the insoluble part of the solution was removed by filtration, and the sample solution was obtained for analysis [35]. After preparing the tap water and fruit samples, we analyzed them using the Co/N-GQDs sensor. In essence, the amalgamation of artificial water and fruit extract involved the incorporation of 100 ng/mL dimethoate. Through this method, the presence of spiked dimethoate in both artificial water and fruits was successfully detected. In conclusion, the dimethoate recovery percentage was calculated based on the slope and intercept of the obtained data. This allowed us to evaluate the sensor’s ability to detect dimethoate under real-world conditions. The results obtained from these actual samples provide valuable insight into the sensor’s practicality and performance under realistic conditions.

The selectivity of fluorescent Co/N-GQDs nanosensors was evaluated for dimethoate detection. Several interfering agents, including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, thiram, glyphosate, malathion, acephate, monocrotophos, methyl parathion, and carbendazim were considered for the interference test. Similarly, stock solutions of metal ions and pesticides (100 ng/mL) were separately prepared in a phosphate buffer with a pH of 7.4 for interference experiments. To elaborate, 5 mL (100 ng/mL) of Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs solution was mixed individually with 1000 μL of such interfering chemical solutions. Following a 15-min incubation period (with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm), fluorescence emission spectra were captured at 461 nm for each sample. Furthermore, the simultaneous presence of all interfering agents, along with dimethoate, was explored to amplify the luminescence intensity of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes.

In the present study, a green approach was used to synthesize Co/N-GQDs from Pithecellobium dulce fruit peel, utilizing a Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal reactor. Notably, the fruit peel powder served as the foundation for the graphitic structure within the hydrothermal reactor under high-pressure and high-temperature conditions, while the ‘Co’ and ‘N’ atoms were introduced as doping agents onto the graphitic framework. The co-doping of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ resulted in enhanced electrical and optical properties of the GQDs. The synthesis process was fine-tuned by varying the concentration of materials and the duration of the reaction, leading to substantial alterations in the fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized batches of Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe. The optimal parameters for the production of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe were determined based on these observations. The fluorescence spectrum was examined at both fixed and varying wavelengths to analyze the behavior centered around excitation. In essence, the production of Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes using eco-friendly techniques proved to be highly sensitive to dimethoate recognition. Additionally, the fabrication of the Co/N-GQDs sensor revealed the definitive optimal variables for production. Leveraging these findings, the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe was exploited as a luminescent sensor for dimethoate recognition.

In this typical experiment, the UV–Vis spectrum of bare GQDs, and Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe revealed the electronic state of as-synthesized materials. Fig. 2
A displays the UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs, Co/N-GQDs nanocomposite. The synthesized bare GQDs reveal a robust absorption peak at 280 nm, demonstrating the π-π* transition (-C = C-), whereas the shoulder peak at 342 nm exhibits a weak n-π* transition of the –C = O- bond. This proves that GQDs can be synthesized from peel powder [36]. Furthermore, the introduction of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ in the graphitic framework of GQDs showcased a high absorption peak at 243 nm and a tail peak at 339 nm, signifying a π-π* transition (-C = C-) and an n-π* transition (-C = O, -C = N-), respectively. From this, a displacement of the peak position was observed compared to pristine GQDs, possibly due to the incorporation of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ ions into the graphitic structure of GQDs [37]. As a result, the successful integration of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ on the surface of GQDs was verified. This demonstrated the proficient synthesis of Co/N-doped GQDs using the hydrothermal technique [31].

During a standard visual inspection, we subjected the green synthesized GQDs and Co/N-GQDs to various wavelengths in a UV cabinet to evaluate their fluorescence sensor capabilities ( Fig. 2 B). The bare GQDs solution showed a faint yellow under visible light ( Fig. 2 B -i). Upon exposure to UV light at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 365 nm, the GQDs emit light green fluorescence ( Fig. 2 B -ii) and blue fluorescence ( Fig. 2 B -ii), respectively. In contrast, the doped, green synthesized Co/N-GQDs appeared faint brown under visible light ( Fig. 2 B -iv). Upon treatment with a UV light excitation spectrum of 254 nm, the Co/N-GQDs exhibited significant green luminescence ( Fig. 2 B -v). In addition, upon exposure to UV light at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the Co/N-GQDs emit a vivid blue luminescence ( Fig. 2 B -vi). The difference in fluorescence color between Co/N-GQDs, and green synthesized GQDs can be attributed to the significant photoluminescence characteristics due to the incorporation of chemical groups ‘Co’ and ‘N’ on the GQDs.

The fluorescence spectra of green-produced Co/N-GQDs were analyzed to explore their photonic capabilities. The spectra were recorded across a range of wavelengths, from 290 to 360 nm ( Fig. 2 C). In this study, Co/N-GQD samples (100 ng/mL) were prepared by dispersing them in a phosphate buffer solution with a pH of 7.4. Subsequently, fluorescence spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths. The results showed that Co/N-GQD exhibited a strong fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, with an emission peak at 461 nm. This emission peak corresponds to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl or carboxyl group on the surface and the presence of fluorophores ( Fig. 2 D). Interestingly, the hydrothermally synthesized Co/N-GQD undergoes excitation wavelength-dependent emission, leading to a redshift. Remarkably, variations in the emissive traps and particle size distribution affected the excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the doped GQDs. In conclusion, the presence of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ in codoped GQDs accounted for the peak position at 400 nm, representing the electron affinities of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ at that specific wavelength.

In this study, we systematically examined the effect of pH on the luminescence characteristics of Co/N-GQDs under UV irradiation with a wavelength of 365 nm, maintaining a constant temperature of 25 °C. Our investigation involved measuring the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs over a range of pH levels spanning from 4.0 to 9.0. Intriguingly, it was observed that both acidic (pH 4.0 and 6.0) and basic (pH 8.0 and 9.0) conditions led to a suppression of luminescence, in contrast to the highest fluorescence intensity observed at the neutral pH of 7.4, which remained stable over seven days ( Fig. 3
A). This pH-dependent behavior, characterized by suppression not only under acidic but also basic conditions, indicates the existence of an optimal pH range for luminescence. Considering the above findings, this suggests a relationship between the protonation and deprotonation of carboxylic and amino groups on the surface of Co/N-GQDs. These chemical processes significantly affect the interaction and quenching or recovery performance of Co/N-GQDs upon exposure to specific target analytes [38]. The majority of functional groups were found to undergo protonation under acidic pH conditions, leading to the suppression of fluorescence. In contrast, as the pH increased from 6 to 9, the degree of dissociation of Co/N-GQDs increased, resulting in a significant increase in the number of carboxyl groups in their structure. These carboxyl groups act as fluorophore species, ultimately increasing the luminescence intensity. As a result, Co/N-GQDs at pH 7.4 showed strong intensity with a significantly higher QY of 49.78 %. Similarly, the analysis of Co/N-GQDs solutions with different pH values under UV radiation confirmed the higher luminescence intensity of the samples at pH 7.4 compared with that at pH 4.0, 6.0, 8.0, and 9.0. Interestingly, changes in fluorescence intensity can be visualized in a UV cabinet ( Fig. 3 B). The results obtained showed that pH 7.4 exhibited higher fluorescence intensity than the other solutions at pH 4.0, 6.0, 8.0, and 9.0. Furthermore, the pH-dependent zeta potential was evaluated and depicted in ( Fig. 3 C). The zeta potential of Co/N-GQDs exhibited positivity in the acidic pH range (pH 4.0, and 6.0). However, beyond pH 6.0, the surface charge of Co/N-GQD changed from positive to negative (pH 7.4, 8.0, and 9.0). This transition is likely the result of the protonation and deprotonation of carboxylic and other functional groups [39]. Previous, investigations have noted that the alteration in peak emission positions accompanying different excitation wavelengths depends on a range of pivotal elements, including the surface functional moieties, chemical composition, quantum confinement phenomenon, edge conditions, size influence, conjugated π-domains, and imperfections in the carbon matrix [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
In a standard test, we meticulously examined the particle size, PDI, and zeta potential measurements of Co/N-GQDs using a particle size analyzer. The role of particle size in affirming the shift from macroscopic to nanoscale architecture was of prime importance. Our study revealed that the freshly synthesized Co/N-GQDs displayed a particle size of 11.5 ± 10.2 nm and a PDI of 0.30 ± 0.06 ( Fig. 4
A), notably confirming well-distributed nanosized materials within the medium. To precisely determine the Co/N-GQDs dimensions, an HR-TEM analysis was conducted. Equally significant, the zeta potential, which is crucial for gauging surface charge and stability, was assessed. Remarkably, the zeta potential of Co/N-GQDs was −25.60 mV (pH 7.4) ( Fig. 4 B), suggesting the presence of chemical moieties like hydroxyl, carboxyl, epoxy, etc., in the synthesized Co/N-GQDs. This zeta potential underscored the high stability of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes.

To evaluate the incorporation of functional moieties into the synthesized GQDs and Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes, an examination was carried out using the FT-IR spectrum. The FT-IR spectra of the synthesized GQDs depicted in Fig. 5
A unveiled distinct absorption peaks at 3369 cm−1, 2935 cm−1, 1633 cm−1, 1584 cm−1, and 1190 cm−1. These peaks are attributed to stretching vibrations of various chemical groups: OH, -C–H, C = O, C = C, and C-O-C. After careful comparison with well-documented references, the data presented confidently confirmed the presence of carboxylic functional groups decorating the surface of GQDs, thus confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs [41]. Upon closer inspection of the FT-IR spectrum of Co/N-GQDs, Fig. 5 B reveals several absorption peaks. The strong absorption peaks at 3443 and 3035 cm−1 with peaks at 1705, 1651, and 1565 cm−1 confirm the existence of OH, –CH, C = O, -C–H, and -C = C chemical moieties. Furthermore, absorption peaks corresponding to stretching vibrations of C − N, C − O, and C − O − C bonds were observed at 1488, 1268, and 1182 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the absorption peak at 668 cm−1 confirmed the presence of ‘Co’ metal ions. FT-IR analysis established the existence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and cobalt functionalities in the overall Co/N-GQDs. Interestingly, the peak intensities in these spectra were found to be reduced compared to bare GQDs, possibly indicating the incorporation of ‘Co’ ions into the graphitic framework. Taken together, these findings confirm the successful synthesis of Co/N-GQDs [42]. In order to further investigate the coordination interaction between Co/N-GQDs and Cu2+, we analyzed the FT-IR spectrum of Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs. Fig. 5 C reveals an important observation, wherein a notable stretching vibration at 1182 cm−1 in Co/N-GQDs disappears in Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs. This disappearance strongly indicates the formation of bonds between Co/N-GQDs and Cu2+ ions.

The configuration of the synthesized Co/N-GQDs nanoprobes was characterized using SEM. Similarly, the chemical composition of the bare GQDs and Co/N-GQDs was verified through EDAX analysis. Fig. 6
A presents an SEM image depicting Co/N-GQDs in an irregular cubic form. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6 B, the elemental composition analysis of the pristine GQDs revealed that they consisted of 48.4 wt% carbon (C) and 51.6 wt% oxygen (O). This finding confirms the conversion of the Pithecellobium dulce fruit shell into a graphitic structure [41]. In Fig. 6 C, the EDAX analysis of Co/N-GQDs reveals the presence of carbon (C) at 27.6 wt%, nitrogen (N) at 19.2 wt%, oxygen (O) at 33.7 wt%, and cobalt (Co) at 19.5 %. The presence of these elements attests to the high purity of Co/N-GQDs. Interestingly, the coexistence of both ‘Co’ and ‘N’ in GQDs indicates dual doping within the graphitic structure. Consequently, the elemental composition of the Co/N-GQDs confirms their intentional design as dual-doped GQDs.

The chemical composition and elemental content of Co/N-GQDs were thoroughly investigated through XPS spectroscopy. Fig. 7
A illustrates the survey scan results, revealing distinct peaks at 284.7 eV (C1s), 400 eV (N1s), 531 eV (O1s), and 780.5 eV (Co 2p). These peaks correspond to carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and cobalt (Co) with atomic percentages of 65.09 %, 4.93 %, 26.27 %, and 3.7 %, respectively. These findings provide valuable insights into both the elemental composition and binding energies of Co/N-GQDs. The 'Cls' deconvoluted high-resolution peaks provided valuable insight (Fig. 7 B), revealing binding energies of 284.7, 286.46, and 288.78 eV for C = C, C-O/C-N, and O-C = O, respectively. Fig. 7 C further sheds light on the 'O1s' XPS spectra, displaying intensity peaks with binding energies of 531.71 eV for C-OH and 532.82 eV for C-O-C. Moving forward, Fig. 7 D showcased the high-resolution 'N1s' peaks, which exhibited binding energies of 399.76 eV for -C-N-C, 400.58 eV for -N-C3, and 401.82 eV for N–H. This robustly confirmed the integration of nitrogen (N) into the graphitic structure of GQDs. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p ( Fig. 7 E) reveal distinct peaks at binding energies 780.67, and 796.64 eV, corresponding to Co 2p 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2, respectively. Additionally, characteristic satellite peaks for Co 2p 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2 are evident at binding energies 788.82 and 803.47 eV, respectively. This observation confirms the existence of Co2+ species [43]. Taken together, these compelling results provide strong support for the existence of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ dopants in the Co/N-GQDs backbone, explaining the fruitful doping of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ elements in the graphitic framework.

In this study, we employed PXRD analysis to ensure the successful fabrication of the graphitic architecture of Co/N-GQDs. Fig. 8
A revealed the diffractogram of Co/N-GQDs, displaying a distinctive wide diffraction peak observed at 2θ = 32.49° and 41.70°. This unequivocally verified the presence of Co/N-GQDs in the amorphous form of a graphene-like structure. The incorporation of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ components into the graphitic structure significantly augmented the lattice voids and induced structural defects. Such unique modifications are expected to play a crucial role in enhancing the material's properties and potential applications. Notably, the SAED patterns portrayed in Fig. 9
B present diffuse rings, further emphasizing the amorphous character of the synthesized material. This observation correlates with the outcomes of XRD patterns, as both techniques confirm the amorphous structural nature [44].

Raman spectroscopy is an efficient, potent, and non-destructive technique to evaluate the structural characterization of graphitic materials and the manufactured excellence of GQDs. In essence, the distinctive D and G band features divulge vital insights into the triumphant formation of the intended quantum dots. Intriguingly, these bands interconnect with hexagonal lattice disorder (D band) and vibrations stemming from sp2 carbon atoms (G band). Utilizing the intensities' proportion within these bands (ID/IG ratio) serves as a means to convey the sp2/sp3 hybridization extent in carbon atoms [45]. The depiction in Fig. 8 B portrays the Raman spectrum of Co/N-GQDs, spotlighting the 'D' band and the 'G' band resonating at 1322 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1, correspondingly. This unambiguously assures carbon's presence within the notably disordered structure of Co/N-GQDs. Remarkably, the calculated ID/IG ratio, standing at 1.15, lays bare evidence of structural imperfections within the framework, originating from the bond distances of C-N and C-Co. Noteworthy is the detected redshift, offering insight into the markedly disordered arrangement attributed to ‘Co’ and ‘N’ doping, thereby compellingly corroborating the triumphant amalgamation of carbon–nitrogen (C-N) and carbon-cobalt (C-Co) bonds [46].

The meticulously fabricated Co/N-GQDs were subjected to a comprehensive analysis using HR-TEM and SAED methods to evaluate their structural characteristics and size distribution. In Fig. 9 A, the HR-TEM image illustrates the spherical configuration of Co/N-GQDs, ranging in size from 2 nm to 20 nm, with an average particle size of 5.17 ± 0.5 nm ( Fig. 9 D). Notably, a solitary nanoparticle exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm in its HR-TEM depiction in Fig. 9 B, resembling the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (sp2). The SAED patterns of the Co/N-GQDs (depicted in Fig. 9 C) revealed diffused rings, indicating the amorphous nature of the manufactured nanomaterial. The presence of this amorphous structure in the Co/N-GQDs validated the success of their fabrication process. Consequently, these investigations provided substantial evidence that the peel of the Pithecellobium dulce fruit could indeed be efficiently repurposed as a carbon source for the production of blue luminescent GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of dimethoate using Co/N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In the context of dimethoate fluorescence sensing, Fig. 10
A clearly shows a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity after the introduction of Cu2+ ions, a phenomenon commonly known as the “Turn-Off” effect, as shown in Fig. S1A. This phenomenon becomes increasingly pronounced with the progressive addition of Cu2+ ions, especially in the concentration range of 1 ng/mL to 900 ng/mL, as depicted in Fig. S1B. Similarly, Fig. S2A further illustrates this phenomenon, showing a gradual decrease in photoluminescence with a concurrent decrease in UV–Vis absorption intensity. To deepen our understanding of the intricate processes underlying the fluorescence quenching of Co/N-GQDs, we meticulously analyzed the FT-IR spectra of Co/N-GQDs, both before and after the introduction of Cu2+ (as depicted in Fig. 5 C). Significantly, the conspicuous disappearance of the stretching vibration peak at 1182 cm−1 in Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs indicates the formation of bonds between oxygen-containing groups of GQDs, which serve as electron donors, and Cu2+ ions, culminating in the creation of a non-fluorescent complex between Cu2+ and Co/N-GQDs. Moreover, we established a clear relationship between Cu2+ ion concentrations, particularly within the range of 10 ng/mL to 800 ng/mL, exhibiting a linear trend (R2 = 0.9968) concerning the fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe, as portrayed in Fig. 10 B. In addition, the proposed system, using quenched Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs, shows fluorescence recovery over a spectrum of dimethoate concentrations from 1 ng/mL to 900 ng/mL (as shown in Fig. S1C and D). Similarly, Fig. S2 B further highlights this phenomenon, revealing a gradual recovery in photoluminescence along with a simultaneous increase in UV–Vis absorption intensity. This correlation corresponds to the subsequent fluorescence recovery observed within the quenched Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs system, as depicted in Fig. 10 C. This observed range exhibits a strong linear correlation (R2 = 0.9986) between the dimethoate concentrations, spanning from 10 ng/mL to 800 ng/mL, as illustrated in Fig. 10 D. After careful calculations, we determined the LOD and LOQ values to be 64.08 ng/mL and 213.6 ng/mL, respectively. This thorough evaluation of dimethoate detection, facilitated through the use of the highly fluorescent Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe, undeniably demonstrates fluorescence recovery within the ‘ng’ concentration range, thereby confirming its outstanding sensitivity. Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of the dimethoate recognition achieved using various previously established sensing approaches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Co/N-GQDs by Cu2+ and recovery by dimethoate
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence quenching mechanisms encompass static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and the inner filter effect [49]. In brief, dynamic quenching involves energy or charge transfer, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity due to collisions between Co/N-GQDs and the quencher, leading to shorter fluorescence lifetimes. In contrast, static quenching arises from interactions between Co/N-GQDs and quencher, yielding non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent species with minimal changes in the excited states of Co/N-GQDs. Fig. 11 A clearly shows a schematic illustration of dimethoate sensing. The operating mechanism of the proposed “On-Off-On” sensing system was investigated using, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence lifetime analysis. Initially, we investigated the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Co/N-GQDs by Cu2+ through fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 11 B, Co/N-GQDs emit at 461 nm under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Upon the introduction of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs is significantly quenched without any shift in the emission peak. In contrast, the presence of dimethoate does not induce significant changes in the fluorescence emission spectra of Co/N-GQDs, indicating limited direct interaction between Co/N-GQDs and dimethoate. However, adding dimethoate to the quenched Cu2+–Co/N-GQD system results in a substantial recovery of fluorescence intensity. Importantly, the shape of the emission spectra of Co/N-GQDs remains largely unaltered, suggesting that the recovered emission primarily originates from Co/N-GQDs rather than from the formation of new complexes. This analysis underscores the distinct fluorescence behavior of Co/N-GQDs in the presence of Cu2+ and dimethoate, emphasizing the effectiveness of this system in detecting dimethoate. To further investigate the quenching mechanism of Co/N-GQDs, UV–Vis spectroscopy was employed to elucidate the quenching process. As illustrated in Fig. 11 C, a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity is observed when Co/N-GQDs are exposed to Cu2+. This reduction in fluorescence is attributed to the dynamic quenching phenomenon, arising from specific coordination interactions between Cu2+ ions and the carboxyl and hydroxy functional groups on the nanomaterial's surface. These interactions play a pivotal role in facilitating intramolecular nonradiative recombination of charge carriers, leading to the partial transfer of an electron to the d orbital of the Cu2+ ion in the excited state [50]. This energy transfer results in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs, resulting in shortened fluorescence lifetimes. To restore the fluorescence signal, dimethoate is subsequently introduced into the quenched Cu2+–Co/N-GQDs system. Notably, dimethoate, characterized by chelating functional groups such as amides and phosphorodithioates, readily forms complex compounds with Cu2+ [51]. This disruption leads to the recovery of fluorescence intensity in Co/N-GQDs. In summary, it is worth noting that dimethoate does not affect the adsorption of Co/N-GQDs. However, when Cu2+ and dimethoate are present simultaneously, the experimental absorption curve of Cu2+-dimethoate (as shown in Fig. 11
C the green curve) around 265 nm is significantly different from that of individual Cu2+ or dimethoate, as shown in Fig. 11 C (cyan and orange curves). In brief, the green curve of Cu2+-dimethoate exhibits an enhanced corresponding π-π* absorption band, confirming the strong interaction between Cu2+ and dimethoate. Ultimately, changes in the UV–Vis spectrum can be effectively monitored to detect and quantify the presence of dimethoate. Furthermore, to confirm the fluorescence dynamic quenching of Co/N-GQDs induced by Cu2+ we conducted fluorescence lifetime measurements for Co/N-GQDs, Cu2+–Co/N-GQD, and Cu2+–Co/N-GQD-dimethoate. As depicted in Fig. 11 D, the fluorescence decay of Co/N-GQDs is accurately described by a three-exponential decay function. Table S1 reports that the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs exhibits three-exponential decay kinetics, each corresponding to a specific photoluminescence (PL) decay time. The calculated average fluorescence lifetimes for Co/N-GQDs, Cu2+–Co/N-GQD, and Cu2+–Co/N-GQD-dimethoate are 14.28 ns, 11.98 ns, and 13.42 ns, respectively. Notably, the presence of Cu2+ has a noticeable impact, causing a reduction in the average lifetime of Co/N-GQDs. This observation confirms that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Co/N-GQDs by Cu2+ follows the dynamic quenching model. Therefore, based on the compelling evidence presented, we can confidently conclude that the quenching of Co/N-GQDs fluorescence induced by Cu2+ is indeed attributed to dynamic quenching.

The selectivity of the designed fluorescent Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe was rigorously assessed in the presence of various potential interfering agents, as visually demonstrated in Fig. 12
A. This figure effectively portrays the fluorescence behavior of the nanoprobe and emphasizes its exceptional ability to recover its fluorescence signal when exposed to dimethoate. Our evaluation primarily focused on the nanoprobe’s capability to selectively detect dimethoate among a spectrum of potential interfering agents, including metal ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cd2+, as well as various pesticides, including thiram, glyphosate, malathion, acephate, monocrotophos, methyl parathion, and carbendazim. Significantly, among these interfering agents, only Cu2+ exhibited a substantial quenching effect on the fluorescence of the nanoprobe, whereas Fe3+ ions displayed a minor quenching effect, likely attributable to a known paramagnetic interaction [52]. Remarkably, even when all these interfering agents were combined in sample Q, they failed to induce fluorescence recovery in the nanoprobe. This underscores the exceptional anti-interference capabilities of the nanoprobe. These findings strongly support the nanoprobe’s potential practicality for accurately detecting dimethoate in both tap water and fruits. Furthermore, a comprehensive assessment of the fluorescence stability of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe (as depicted in Fig. 12 B) was undertaken. This meticulous analysis yielded a pleasing result, with a calculated % RSD of 1.35, notably well below the 5 % threshold. This outcome unequivocally affirms the robust stability of the synthesized Co/N-GQDs over 6 days. The meticulous examination of the stability and reproducibility of the proposed fluorescent Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe for evaluating dimethoate content is elegantly presented in Fig. 12 C and D, respectively. The calculated % RSD values stood at 0.39 % and 1.35 % for stability and, reproducibility respectively. Additionally, the assessment of dimethoate concentrations (at 100 ng/mL) was diligently conducted in both artificial water and fruit samples using the quenched nanoprobe, as shown in Table 2 . shows that the recovery rate and RSD for dimethoate ranged from 95.16 to 95.66 % and 0.81 to 1.79 %, respectively. These findings collectively underscore the authenticity and practical applicability of the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe-based approach for real-time dimethoate quantification, thereby validating its promising potential in analytical contexts.

In this study, we designed a facile and highly sensitive “On-Off-On” fluorescent sensor for the recognition of dimethoate. This sensor relies on a Co/N-GQD-Cu2+ nanoprobes system. Our designed fluorescence sensor has demonstrated exceptional selectivity in the detection of dimethoate in real samples. To synthesize the Co/N-GQDs, we adopted a one-pot hydrothermal method, using Pithecellobium dulce fruit peel as a precursor. Additionally, ‘Co’ and urea were utilized as dopants. The Co/N-GQDs synthesized through this method exhibited an impressive absolute quantum yield of 49.78 %. Spectroscopic analysis has confirmed the effective incorporation of ‘Co’ and ‘N’ into the graphitic structure, resulting in a significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity compared to its components. In essence, the fluorescence intensity of Co/N-GQDs is selectively quenched (“Turn-Off”) by Cu2+ through a dynamic quenching mechanism. When dimethoate is included in the quenching system, a proportional correlation is observed between the concentration of dimethoate and the reactivation of fluorescence. The resulting nanoprobe has demonstrated an impressive detection sensitivity, achieving an exceptionally low limit of 64.08 ng/mL, while also showcasing robust stability and outstanding selectivity for dimethoate, even in the presence of potential interferents. We further validated the applicability of this luminescent sensing platform through spiking experiments conducted in water and fruit samples. In conclusion, the Co/N-GQDs nanoprobe provides a sensitive, straightforward, and selective approach with significant potential for the cost-effective and sustainable detection of dimethoate in both water and food matrices. This study advances eco-friendly sensing and opens new possibilities for pesticide sensing.

Mahendra R. Mahajan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Pravin O. Patil: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.
